data.title: OPENING SCENE
blocks: Here is a project that happens in real life: a researcher needs a briefing note summarizing recent literature on a speci
Opening Scene — Without Claude, the researcher searches databases, reads abstracts, builds the matrix by hand, drafts the note, edits it

S03.meta: // SLIDE 03 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Decompose a real project into tasks and route each task to the correct Claude surface
· Set up a project workspace, write task packet specifications, and run approval gates
· Collect verification evidence at each hand-off
· Produce an AI-use log and a reflection that transfers to your next project
· Recognize when to use all three surfaces and when to use fewer
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

S04.meta: // SLIDE 04 — BEFORE YOU BEGIN: PROJECT SELE

data.title: BEFORE YOU BEGIN: PROJECT SELECTION
blocks: · Small enough to complete in a few hours
· Rich enough to need at least two Claude surfaces
Real enough that the output matters a document you will actually use, share, or submit
· Safe enough that you are not routing genuinely sensitive, proprietary, or human-subjects data through AI systems under t
· A briefing note on a topic in your field for a non-specialist audience

S05.meta: // SLIDE 05 — THE WORKED EXAMPLE: A RESEARCH

data.title: THE WORKED EXAMPLE: A RESEARCH BRIEFING NOTE
blocks: · What should Claude produce?
· What context does Claude need?
· What are the constraints?
· What will I verify before using the output?
· Does the citation match a real document in your folder?

data.title: THE CAPSTONE ARTIFACT BUNDLE.
blocks: Project brief one paragraph, including the data/privacy boundary note
Surface-routing map one table, with rationale for each routing decision
Task packet specifications one packet per AI-assisted task
AI-use log one table, with gate checks and actions taken
Verification checklist the human-gate checks you ran (sources, calculations, claims, figures)

data.title: CONSTRAINED VERSION: CHAT-ONLY SIMULATION
blocks: · Replace Cowork file assembly with manual copy-paste from verified sources into a structured table you build yourself
· Replace Claude Code scripts with manual spreadsheet work or find-and-replace
· Keep all four gates; they apply in chat-only workflows too
· The project brief and boundary note
· The task packet specification for each chat session

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Treating the final artifact as the capstone deliverable.
Using all three surfaces because the chapter says to.
Letting Cowork or Code run to completion before reviewing intermediate outputs.
Asking Claude to verify its own work.
Writing a reflection that describes what happened without transferring to future practice.

S09.meta: // SLIDE 09 — THE HUMAN GATE IN THE CAPSTONE

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE IN THE CAPSTONE.
blocks: Every chapter in this book returned to the same idea: the human gate is where the work becomes trustworthy. The capstone
The Human Gate in the Capstone — In chat, the gate is the task packet review and the scope clarification before you generate anything consequential.
data.section: THE HUMAN GATE IN THE CAPSTONE

S10.meta: // SLIDE 10 — CLOSING: THE LAST HUMAN GATE

data.title: CLOSING: THE LAST HUMAN GATE
blocks: You opened this book at a polished artifact that might not be trustworthy. Chapters 1 through 9 gave you a set of tools
Closing: The Last Human Gate — This chapter asked you to use all of them, once, on something real.
